topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crumpled paper felt like a lead weight in my hand. It was my essay, the one I’d poured weeks of agonizing introspection into, now displayed on a glossy webpage under the heading, "Illustrative Examples for Aspiring Scholars." My breath hitched, a shallow, panicked thing in my chest. The words, my deeply personal narrative about navigating a difficult summer internship, were perfectly polished, eloquent, and undeniably compelling. They belonged to someone else now, a template for future hopefuls. I quickly shoved the printout deep into my backpack, my fingers trembling as I zipped the compartment shut. Walking across campus, I kept my gaze fixed on the pavement, the noise of students and traffic sounding muffled, distant. Every glance from a passing classmate felt like an accusation, a spotlight illuminating the embarrassing gap between my hopeful ambition and this stark reality.

[story 2]

I found it tucked inside a discarded stack of discarded syllabi in the university library annex. The screen glowed, displaying my carefully crafted prose—a story about the unexpected resilience I found while volunteering at the local animal shelter. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward like spilled ink. I leaned closer, my knuckles whitening as I traced the lines of the published piece. The language was so effortlessly mature, so perfectly structured. A wave of intense heat flushed my neck and cheeks. I snatched the paper, my movements jerky and clumsy, and retreated to a quiet corner of the study room. I stared at the screen, my mouth dry, feeling the weight of expectation settle heavily on my shoulders, a crushing burden of inadequacy.

[story 3]

It happened while browsing an online portfolio of successful applicants. My eyes snagged on a title that made my heart skip a beat: "The Quiet Strength of Adaptation." Beneath it was my essay. A sudden, sharp intake of breath stole the air from my lungs. I felt a profound sense of exposure, as if the very air around me had thickened, pressing in. My hands started to shake uncontrollably, and I quickly minimized the window, my posture immediately shrinking inward. I stood up abruptly, needing to move, needing distance. The hallway seemed suddenly too bright, too loud. I walked quickly toward the exit, the sound of my own hurried footsteps echoing unnervingly loud in the sudden silence of my internal panic.
